Searched the web

Laljeet, this is a really sharp idea. Let me lay out what I'm seeing and then help you think through how to frame this.

The flyer you photographed shows Bakersfield's proposed sewer rate schedule: single-family residential goes from $247.13 currently to $475 in FY 26/27, then $100/year increases reaching $875 by FY 30/31. That's roughly a 254% increase over five years. The AECOM study and HF&H cost-of-service study justified this based on the ~$500-600M needed to replace the 1950s-era Planz Road treatment plant, plus deferred maintenance on sewer lines, lift stations, and storm drains.

The city's talking point, as you noted, is that Bakersfield's sewer rate has been kept significantly lower than similar sized cities for decades Turnto23 and the City Manager stated the city is three times lower than most average cities in their costs for a similar service Turnto23. But your point is exactly right: that comparison is meaningless without normalizing by income. Bakersfield's median household income is about $82,093, roughly 80% of California's median of $100,149 Census Reporter, and per capita income is about $33,380, roughly two-thirds of the state figure Census Reporter. And there's enormous variation within Bakersfield: zip codes like 93305 have a median household income of just $43,244 Income by Zipcode, while 93311 sits at $116,263 Income by Zipcode. The poverty rate is about 15%, roughly 1.3 times the national rate Census Reporter.

So applying the Maxcy-Brown et al. (2024) framework here makes a lot of sense. Their key methodological contributions that translate directly to your idea:

The USEPA's 2.5% of MHI threshold applied to Bakersfield tells a striking story. At the proposed $875/year endpoint, a household needs to earn at least $35,000/year for sewer alone to remain "affordable." That excludes water charges entirely. The Bakersfield water rate is also increasing (34% first year). Combined water and sewer at the 4.5% MHI threshold tells an even more concerning story for households in the lower-income census tracts.

The real power of their method, which you could replicate for Bakersfield, is the income bracket analysis at the census tract level rather than relying on citywide MHI. Using MHI alone, Bakersfield might look fine. Using the income distribution within each tract exposes how many actual households face unaffordable bills, exactly the same masking effect they demonstrated in Alabama.

A few directions this could go:

You could frame this as a companion affordability analysis for the Prop 218 process, applying the Maxcy-Brown methodology to Kern County or Bakersfield specifically. The data infrastructure is very similar to your consolidation work: ACS tract-level income brackets, sewer rate data from the city, and spatial overlays. You could also bring in the water side since many Bakersfield residents pay both water and sewer to the city, and the combined burden is what matters.

The policy guardrails angle is compelling. The city is required under Prop 218 to demonstrate that fees don't exceed the cost of service, but there is no affordability test built into that process. Your study could quantify how many households will cross the 2.5% threshold at each step of the rate schedule, giving the council and community concrete numbers rather than abstract comparisons to other cities.

Want me to help you sketch out a quick analysis framework or start pulling together the census tract income data for the Bakersfield sewer service area? Or are you thinking more about this as a commentary/op-ed piece versus a full analytical study?

I 'm thinking this of short structred study. Like reaecrh letters. Mainly i 'm goign to post it more as seeing what will happen this may not go into journal

3:17 PM

Got it. A focused research letter or brief communication, structured enough to be credible but lean enough that it stands on its own as a public-facing analysis. Something you could post on GitHub, share with the council, or submit to a journal if it gains traction.

Here's how I'd think about structuring it:

The core argument is straightforward: the city justifies rate increases by comparing absolute rates to other cities, but affordability is a function of rates relative to income. Applying established USEPA affordability thresholds and census tract-level income data to the proposed Bakersfield sewer rate schedule reveals the actual household burden, and it's not evenly distributed.

For structure, you could do something like: brief introduction framing the Prop 218 process and the rate schedule, a methods section describing the USEPA 2.5% threshold applied to ACS income bracket data by census tract (citing Maxcy-Brown et al. 2024 as the methodological basis), results showing the number and percentage of households exceeding the threshold at each step of the rate schedule (current through FY 30/31), and a short discussion noting that the "lower than average rates" argument collapses when income is considered, plus what guardrails could look like (phased assistance programs, income-based tiers, etc.).

The analysis itself is pretty compact. You need the proposed rate schedule (which you have from the flyer), ACS tract-level income bracket data for the Bakersfield sewer service area, and then the same linear interpolation approach Maxcy-Brown used to estimate households below the affordability threshold at each rate step. You could also layer in the water rate increases to show the combined burden at the 4.5% threshold.

Want me to start pulling the ACS data and setting up the R script to run this? I can structure it to match your GitHub workflow and your writing preferences.

Good. Bakersfield city FIPS is 0603526, Kern County FIPS is 06029. Now let me build the scripts. I'll split into: (1) data acquisition, (2) affordability analysis, (3) visualization.

Created 4 files

Now let me copy everything to outputs so you can download the project.

Ran 2 commands, read a file

Here's the project structure with three scripts:

Script 01 (Data Acquisition) pulls ACS Table B19001 (income brackets) and B19013 (MHI) at the census tract level for Kern County, then spatially clips tracts to the Bakersfield city boundary using tigris::places(). Split tracts get area-weighted household counts. You'll need a Census API key (tidycensus::census_api_key()).

Script 02 (Affordability Analysis) implements the Maxcy-Brown linear interpolation method. For each rate step ($247 through $875), it calculates the income threshold (rate / 0.025), then walks through each tract's income brackets to count households below that threshold. It also runs the traditional MHI-based analysis side-by-side so you can directly show how MHI masks the problem.

Script 03 (Visualization) generates five figures: a bar chart of % unaffordable households across the rate schedule, the MHI vs. bracket-based comparison, two maps (bracket-based and MHI-based at the $875 endpoint), and a burden-by-income-bracket chart showing where the 2.5% threshold falls.

A few things to consider as you run this:

The sewer service area approximation using city limits is imperfect. The city serves about 165,000 people according to news coverage, but the service area extends somewhat beyond city limits in places and excludes areas served by East Niles CSD and other districts. You could note this as a limitation or refine the boundary if you can get the actual service area shapefile from the city.

The rate schedule from your flyer shows the "multiple dwelling" rate is lower ($185.35 current, reaching $656.25 by FY 30/31). You could add that as a second scenario since apartment dwellers tend to have lower incomes.

The water rate increases layer on top. If you want the combined water+sewer analysis at the 4.5% threshold, that's a straightforward extension of Script 02.

## Reshape income data to long format for analysis income_long <- tracts_in_city %>% + st_drop_geometry() %>% + select(GEOID, NAME, overlap_frac, + B19001_001E, # total households + paste0("B19001_", sprintf("%03d", 2:17), "E")) %>% # bracket estimates + rename(total_hh = B19001_001E) %>% + pivot_longer( + cols = starts_with("B19001_") & ends_with("E") & !matches("001"), + names_to = "variable", + values_to = "hh_count" + ) %>% + mutate( + variable = str_remove(variable, "E$"), + # Weight household counts by overlap fraction + hh_count_weighted = hh_count * overlap_frac, + total_hh_weighted = total_hh * overlap_frac + ) %>% + left_join(bracket_info, by = "variable") Error in pivot_longer(): ! cols must select at least one column. Run rlang::last_trace() to see where the error occurred.

3:26 PM

The issue is that the select() with paste0() is consuming the columns before pivot_longer can find them, or the column names don't match the pattern after selection. Let me check what the actual column names look like from get_acs() with output = "wide".

Check the current script to fix the pivot_longer issue

The problem is that after rename(total_hh = B19001_001E), the remaining B19001 columns still have the E suffix, but the pivot_longer selector starts_with("B19001_") & ends_with("E") doesn't find them because select() with paste0() inside tidy evaluation can be tricky. The cleaner fix is to avoid mixing paste0 in select and use explicit column references instead.

Edited a file, ran a command, read a file

The fix: paste0() inside select() doesn't work with tidy evaluation the way you'd expect. The solution defines bracket_cols as a character vector first, then uses all_of(bracket_cols) in both select() and pivot_longer(). That way the column names are resolved explicitly rather than through tidyselect helpers that can't find them after the rename.

3:34 PM

The numbers are clearly wrong. The y-axis on Figs 1 and 2 shows values like 600.5%, 1521.3%, 3030.8%, which are nonsensical for a percentage of households. The issue is that pct_unaffordable is being calculated as a raw number (household count) but displayed with percent_format(scale = 1), or more likely, the calculation itself is producing inflated values.

Let me trace the problem. Looking at Fig 3, every single tract is >= 75% unaffordable (all dark red), which is implausible. And Fig 4 (MHI-based) shows the MHI method finding almost zero unaffordable tracts, with the blue bars in Fig 2 essentially invisible. That combination suggests the income bracket calculation is massively overcounting.

The likely bug is in 02_affordability_analysis.R in the calculate_unaffordable_hh function. The function receives income_long %>% filter(GEOID == .data$GEOID), but since income_long has one row per bracket per tract, and the function sums across brackets, I suspect the issue is that results is joining back to tract_summary and the pct_unaffordable is computed against total_hh_weighted from a single tract, but unaffordable_hh is summing across all tracts due to the rowwise() + filter() interaction not working correctly.

Let me check: with rowwise(), .data$GEOID inside mutate should refer to the current row's GEOID. But actually the problem might be simpler: pct_unaffordable in the summary table is computed as unaffordable_hh / total_hh * 100, but if total_hh is already aggregated differently than unaffordable_hh, you get values over 100%.

Can you paste the console output from the summary table print? Specifically the lines like:

=== SUMMARY: Households with Unaffordable Sewer Access ===

That will tell me whether the per-tract numbers are wrong or just the aggregation. In the meantime, I'm fairly confident the core issue is one of two things. Let me fix both.

Review the affordability calculation script for bugs

I see the problems. There are two bugs:

Bug 1: The pct_unaffordable values (600%, 3030%) are clearly raw household counts being treated as percentages, or the percentage calculation is dividing by something too small. Looking at Fig 1, "600.5%" for the current rate probably means ~600 households are unaffordable out of, say, ~120,000 total, which would be ~0.5%. The pct_unaffordable in the summary is being calculated correctly in formula (unaffordable_hh / total_hh * 100), so the issue must be that unaffordable_hh in the summary is the actual household count (e.g., 600) and it's being displayed as a percentage without further division. Wait, no: 600/total*100 wouldn't give 600%.

Actually, looking more carefully: the values 600.5, 1521.3, 3030.8 look like they could be raw household counts being plotted directly. The summary_table$pct_unaffordable is computed as unaffordable_hh / total_hh * 100. If total_hh is somehow very small (like only counting one tract), that would inflate the percentage.

But the more fundamental issue is Bug 2: the MHI-based method shows essentially 0% at all rates. At $875/yr, the threshold income is $35,000. Any tract with MHI < $35,000 should flag. Bakersfield has tracts with MHIs in the $30-40k range, so there should be some red. The MHI map (Fig 4) does show a few red/orange tracts, so the MHI calculation works, but Fig 2 shows the blue bars as invisible, meaning hh_in_mhi_unaffordable_tracts is near zero relative to the inflated bracket-based number.

The root cause is most likely that pct_unaffordable at the per-tract level is exceeding 100% for many tracts, which then inflates the aggregate. This points back to the interpolation function. Let me rewrite Script 02 with better diagnostics and a cleaner calculation.

Created 2 files, ran a command

Now let me also fix the visualization script so the y-axis formatting works correctly regardless:

Check the fig1 section of visualization script

The percent_format(scale = 1) treats the value as already in percentage points (so 25 becomes "25%"). That's correct if pct_unaffordable is already 0-100. The issue was in the calculation, not the formatting. But let me also add a secondary y-axis label showing household counts, and add the number of affected households to the bar labels since that's more impactful.

Edited a file, ran a command, read a file

Here's what I changed and why:

Three bugs in the original Script 02:

  1. Interpolation boundary condition: The original used bracket_width <- ub - lb + 1, which adds 1 to the denominator. For the $0-$9,999 bracket that's a width of 10,000, so adding 1 is negligible. But the real problem was threshold > ub using > when it should be >=. Combined with how the function was filtering tract data, some households were being double-counted or the fractions were exceeding 1.0 within a bracket.
  2. Denominator mismatch: The original pct_unaffordable divided unaffordable_hh by total_hh_weighted (from B19001_001E, the total row), but unaffordable_hh was summed from the individual bracket estimates. ACS totals and bracket sums don't always match exactly. The fix uses sum_bracket_hh (the actual sum of bracket counts) as the denominator, plus a pmin(., 100) safety cap.
  3. The nest() approach: The original passed income_long %>% filter(GEOID == .data$GEOID) inside rowwise() %>% mutate(), which is fragile with .data$ pronoun scoping inside rowwise contexts. The fix pre-nests the data by tract and passes the nested tibble directly.

Diagnostics added: The new script prints the data structure, a sample tract's bracket distribution, and the distribution of per-tract percentages for each scenario. When you re-run it, paste that diagnostic output and I can confirm the numbers look right before you regenerate the figures.

By FY 30/31 at $875/yr, that jumps to 29,122 households (23.3%), which is strikingly close to Maxcy-Brown's 23.7% finding for Alabama statewide.

The MHI-based method catches only 11 tracts at the highest rate, while the bracket-based method shows the problem is distributed across the city (median tract has 20.9% unaffordable).

can you explain me the differenc ebetween two methods

3:45 PM

Sure. Both methods ask the same question: "How many households can't afford this sewer rate?" The difference is how they count.

MHI-based (traditional method): Take each census tract's single median household income number. Compare the sewer rate to 2.5% of that MHI. If $875/yr is less than 2.5% of the tract's MHI, the entire tract is labeled "affordable." If it exceeds 2.5%, the entire tract is labeled "unaffordable," and all households in that tract get counted.

The problem is that MHI is the 50th percentile. A tract with an MHI of $73,000 gets labeled affordable at $875/yr because $875 is 1.2% of $73,000. But that tract still has households earning $20,000, $30,000, $40,000 for whom $875 represents 4.4%, 2.9%, or 2.2% of income. The MHI method completely ignores them. It only flags a tract when more than half the households are below the threshold, which is an extremely high bar.

That's why you're seeing only 11 tracts flagged by MHI at $875/yr. Those are the tracts with MHI below $35,000 ($875 / 0.025 = $35,000). Bakersfield has very few tracts that poor overall, so MHI says the city is mostly fine.

Income bracket-based (Maxcy-Brown method): Instead of using one summary number per tract, this method uses the ACS Table B19001 income distribution, which reports how many households fall into each of 16 income brackets ($0-$10k, $10k-$15k, $15k-$20k, etc.). For each tract, the method calculates the income threshold below which sewer is unaffordable ($35,000 at $875/yr), then counts the actual households in brackets below that threshold. For the bracket that contains the threshold, it uses linear interpolation to estimate the fraction of households below it.

So in that same tract with $73,000 MHI, maybe 8% of households earn under $15,000, another 5% earn $15-25k, another 7% earn $25-35k. The bracket method counts all of those individually. It finds the 20% of households in that tract who actually face unaffordable bills, even though the tract "looks fine" by MHI.

That's the core argument for your letter: the city says rates are affordable because Bakersfield's MHI can absorb them, but the bracket method reveals that roughly 29,000 households across the service area face unaffordable sewer access at the proposed endpoint rate, and MHI-based analysis misses most of them.

did city do mhi analysis?

3:48 PM

No, not exactly. From what I found in the news coverage, the city's argument is even simpler than MHI analysis. They're comparing absolute dollar rates to other cities. The City Manager's talking point was essentially: our rate is three times lower than comparable cities, so even after the increase we're still cheaper.

The city didn't apply the USEPA 2.5% of MHI threshold at all, as far as I can tell from the public reporting. They did a cost-of-service study (AECOM engineering evaluation, then HF&H rate study) to justify that the fees match the cost of providing sewer service, which is the Prop 218 legal requirement. Prop 218 requires that fees not exceed the cost of service, but it has no affordability test built in.

So your analysis is actually introducing both methods as new contributions to this discussion. Neither the MHI-based approach nor the bracket-based approach was part of the city's rate-setting process. That makes your framing even stronger: the city justified rates purely on a cost comparison to other utilities without any affordability analysis, and when affordability analysis is applied, even the conservative MHI method flags 11 tracts, while the more granular bracket method shows ~23% of households affected.

Do you want me to search for whether the AECOM or HF&H reports are publicly available? That would let you confirm exactly what analysis the city did and didn't do, and cite it directly.

Rates remain below other California cities, including Anaheim and Sacramento, even after an increase in Fiscal Year 2025-26. Since 2006, the City of Bakersfield’s sewer rate has been among the lowest in the state and has only seen modest Consumer Price Index increases since 2022. How will a new rate amount be determined? Adoption of new sewer user rates needs to follow the guidelines outlined in California’s Proposition 218. The City will release and make available the 2026 Sewer Rates and Fees Report, which includes a completed cost-of-service analysis. Property owners affected by changes to the rates will be notified 45-days in advance of a public hearing. During the 45-day window property owners are able to provide a written response in support or opposition to the proposed rates. At the public hearing City Council will vote to either adopt or reject the proposed rates.

3:51 PM

This is exactly the chart your analysis dismantles. They're comparing absolute dollars across cities with wildly different incomes. Bakersfield at $247 vs. San Francisco at $2,000 looks favorable, but San Francisco's MHI is roughly $126,000 while Bakersfield's is around $82,000 (and Kern County overall is $68,000). The rate as a percentage of income tells a completely different story.

You could actually build a version of their chart that normalizes by MHI. Using the cities they chose:

Bakersfield at $875 (proposed endpoint) against an $82k MHI is 1.07%. San Francisco at $2,000 against $126k MHI is 1.59%. Sacramento at $832 against $86k MHI is 0.97%. So Bakersfield at the proposed rate would actually be higher burden than Sacramento, despite the city's chart suggesting otherwise.

And that's still using MHI, which as your analysis shows understates the problem for lower-income households.

Do you want me to write a small script (or add to the existing project) that recreates their comparison chart but adds the income-normalized version side by side? It would make a strong visual for the letter, directly countering their framing with their own city selection. I'd just need to pull MHI for each city they listed.

okay few things. We definetaly want o recreate atleast MHI for other places that they showed. Just for reasonalble comparison. Other hting i 'm thinking is one may say hey income will increase till 2030 so maybe impact will not be taht high

4:01 PM

Good points, both of them. Let me address the income growth concern first since it affects how we frame the analysis, then we can build the comparison chart.

On the income growth argument: that's a fair objection someone could raise. The ACS data is 2019-2023, and the rate schedule runs through FY 30/31. Bakersfield's MHI has been growing, but the question is whether income growth keeps pace with the rate increases. The current rate is $247 and goes to $875, a 254% increase over five years. Household income would need to grow at roughly the same rate to keep the affordability picture stable, and that's not happening anywhere in the country, let alone in Kern County.

What I'd suggest: we can add a sensitivity analysis that inflates the income brackets by some reasonable annual growth rate (say 3% nominal, which is generous for Bakersfield) and re-run the affordability calculation. That lets you say "even under optimistic income growth assumptions, X% of households still face unaffordable access by FY 30/31."

Before I start coding, let me ask: what did you see from the rate_schedule print and the diagnostic output from Script 02? I want to confirm the numbers match what I parsed from the CSV (130 tracts, ~125k households, 23.3% at FY 30/31) before building on top of it.

And for the city comparison chart, do you want me to use all the cities from their chart (Fresno, Kern County KSA, Shafter, NORSD, Anaheim, Oakland, San Jose, Arvin, San Diego, Sacramento, LA, San Francisco), or a subset?

This script produces two side-by-side charts using the exact same cities from the city's comparison graphic.

Fig 6a replicates their absolute dollar chart but adds the proposed FY 30/31 endpoint ($875) in red. Fig 6b normalizes each city's rate by its MHI. The punchline should be striking: City of Arvin at $726.64 against a $49,984 MHI will show up as ~1.45% of MHI, while Bakersfield's proposed $875 against $82,093 comes to ~1.07%. But the really interesting ones will be the SJV comparison cities (Fresno, Shafter, Kern County KSA) where both rates and incomes are low, putting the burden in similar territory to Bakersfield.

04 rate comparison
Code · R 

seperate script

4:09 PM
Created a file, ran a command, read a file

The key math that makes the income growth argument collapse: the rate increases from $247 to $875, a 254% increase over roughly 5 years. Even at an optimistic 5% annual income growth sustained over 9 years (from the ACS midpoint of 2021 to FY 30/31), cumulative income growth would only be about 55%. The rate increase outpaces even the most generous income growth assumption by a factor of roughly 4-5x.

The script tests four scenarios (0%, 2%, 3%, 5% annual growth) and produces a line chart showing the percentage of households with unaffordable access under each assumption. Rather than inflating all 16 bracket bounds for every tract, it uses the mathematically equivalent shortcut of deflating the affordability threshold back to base-year dollars. Same result, much faster.

One thing to note: the years_from_base values assume the ACS 2019-2023 midpoint is roughly 2021. If you want to adjust that assumption, just change those values in the rate_schedule tibble. Let me know what the output looks like when you run it.

That's the right framing. The message isn't "don't raise rates." The message is "stop telling people this is fine when the data shows it's not, and build in protections for the households that can't absorb this."

Before I start building the outreach piece, let me make sure I understand the core points you want to land:

  1. The infrastructure investment is necessary and not in question
  2. The city's "lower than average rates" comparison is misleading because it ignores that Bakersfield has lower than average incomes
  3. When standard USEPA affordability methods are applied, roughly 23% of households face unaffordable sewer access at the proposed endpoint rate
  4. The city did zero affordability analysis as part of the rate-setting process
  5. Guardrails are needed: customer assistance programs, phased relief, income-based considerations

Okay this looks goos i will imbedd figures. Are you sure your code and math is correct? Or do you want me to do another check on my own

4:23 PM

You should definitely check it on your own. There are a few specific things I'd want you to verify:

The interpolation logic. Pick one tract manually and walk through the calculation by hand. Take a tract with a low MHI (maybe one of those 11 tracts flagged by the MHI method) and one with a middle-range MHI. For each, look at the B19001 bracket counts, calculate the threshold at $875/yr ($35,000), and manually sum the households below that threshold with the interpolation. Compare your hand calculation to what the script produces for that tract in the CSV.

The service area household count. The analysis found ~124,900 households across 130 tracts. News coverage says the city serves about 165,000 people. At Bakersfield's average household size of about 2.9-3.0, that would be roughly 55,000-57,000 households, which is actually much lower than 124,900. That discrepancy concerns me. It could mean the city boundary (which I used as a proxy) is much larger than the actual sewer service area, or it could mean the 165,000 figure from news reports is outdated or refers to something else. You're local, so you'd have a better sense of whether 125,000 households sounds right for the service area. The city sent 106,000 Prop 218 notices, which is closer to that number.

The overlap weighting. Check whether the area-based weighting for split tracts is producing sensible numbers, or if some tracts at the city edge are getting over- or under-counted.

The ACS year. I set acs_year = 2023 which pulls 2019-2023 5-year estimates. Confirm that's what actually downloaded on your machine.

The math in the sensitivity script (deflating the threshold instead of inflating brackets) is algebraically equivalent, but worth a sanity check on one scenario.

4:29 PM

That's the better workflow. Here's what to change in Script 01:

Replace the section that pulls the Bakersfield city boundary and does the spatial intersection (roughly lines 60-90) with this:

r
## --- 3. Load sewer service area boundary -------------------------------------
cat("Loading sewer service area boundary...\n")

sewer_boundary <- st_read("path/to/Sewer_Service_Area/")  # point to your .shp

## --- 4. Identify tracts within sewer service area ----------------------------
cat("Identifying tracts within sewer service area...\n")

income_brackets_raw <- st_transform(income_brackets_raw, st_crs(sewer_boundary))

income_brackets_raw$tract_area <- st_area(income_brackets_raw)

tracts_in_city <- st_intersection(income_brackets_raw, sewer_boundary)
tracts_in_city$overlap_area <- st_area(tracts_in_city)

tracts_in_city$overlap_frac <- as.numeric(
  tracts_in_city$overlap_area / tracts_in_city$tract_area
)

tracts_in_city <- tracts_in_city %>%
  filter(overlap_frac >= 0.10)

Also delete the bkfd_boundary lines and the saveRDS(bkfd_boundary, ...) call, and replace with saveRDS(sewer_boundary, "data/sewer_boundary.rds").

In Script 03, replace references to bkfd_boundary with sewer_boundary (or whatever you load it as) for the map background.

Run Script 01, then tell me the new tract count and total household count and I can confirm whether the numbers look reasonable before you re-run the rest.

4:32 PM

139,836 households across 133 tracts. That's higher than the 124,900 from the city boundary proxy, which makes sense since the sewer service area likely extends beyond city limits in some places.

The 106,000 Prop 218 notices went to property owners, not households (renters don't get notices, and some properties are multi-unit). So 139,836 total households served is plausible with that notice count.

Go ahead and run Scripts 02 through 05. The key numbers I'd want to see from Script 02 are the total households, unaffordable households, and percentage for each scenario, plus how many tracts the MHI method flags at FY 30/31. That will tell us if the fact sheet numbers need updating.

in script 2 > cat("=== Data structure check ===\n") === Data structure check === > cat(sprintf(" Unique tracts: %d\n", n_distinct(income_long$GEOID))) Unique tracts: 133 > cat(sprintf(" Rows in income_long: %d\n", nrow(income_long))) Rows in income_long: 2192 > cat(sprintf(" Rows per tract: %d (should be 16)\n", + nrow(income_long) / n_distinct(income_long$GEOID))) Error in sprintf(" Rows per tract: %d (should be 16)\n", nrow(income_long)/n_distinct(income_long$GEOID)) : invalid format '%d'; use format %f, %e, %g or %a for numeric objects

4:33 PM

That's because 2192 / 133 = 16.48, which isn't an integer, so %d fails. Some tracts have more than 16 rows, likely because the spatial intersection split a tract that overlaps multiple parts of the sewer boundary, creating duplicate GEOID entries.

Check this:

r
income_long %>% count(GEOID) %>% filter(n != 16)

That will show which tracts have extra rows. The fix goes in Script 01: after the intersection, add a step to aggregate any duplicate tract fragments before pivoting. Add this right after the filter(overlap_frac >= 0.10) line:

r
## If a tract intersects multiple polygons in the service area boundary,
## combine the fragments by summing overlap fractions (capped at 1.0)
tracts_in_city <- tracts_in_city %>%
  group_by(GEOID) %>%
  summarise(
    across(starts_with("B19001_"), first),
    NAME = first(NAME),
    overlap_frac = min(sum(overlap_frac), 1.0),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

The sewer service area shapefile probably has multiple polygons (one per service zone or treatment plant catchment), and a tract sitting on the boundary of two zones gets duplicated. This aggregation step merges those back into one row per tract.

Re-run Script 01 with that fix and confirm you get exactly 16 rows per tract.

income_long %>% count(GEOID) %>% filter(n != 16) # A tibble: 4 × 2 GEOID n <chr> <int> 1 06029000507 32 2 06029001801 32 3 06029002403 32 4 06029003127 32 what's going on here idint understand it

4:34 PM

Four tracts each have exactly 32 rows (16 x 2). That confirms what I suspected: the sewer service area shapefile has multiple polygons (probably separate service zones), and those four tracts straddle two polygons. So the intersection creates two copies of each tract, each with its own overlap fraction.

Think of it this way: tract 06029000507 sits partly in Zone A and partly in Zone B of the sewer service area. The intersection gives you one fragment for the Zone A overlap and one for the Zone B overlap, each with 16 income bracket rows. The household counts get duplicated.

The fix in Script 01 merges those fragments back into one row per tract before the pivot. Add this right after filter(overlap_frac >= 0.10):

r
## Merge tract fragments that were split by multiple service area polygons
tracts_in_city <- tracts_in_city %>%
  group_by(GEOID) %>%
  summarise(
    across(starts_with("B19001_"), first),
    NAME         = first(NAME),
    tract_area   = first(tract_area),
    overlap_frac = min(sum(overlap_frac), 1.0),
    .groups      = "drop"
  )

The first() calls are safe because the B19001 data and NAME are identical across fragments (they come from the same tract). Only the overlap fraction differs, and summing those gives the total fraction of the tract inside the service area, capped at 1.0.

Re-run Script 01 and check that income_long %>% count(GEOID) %>% filter(n != 16) returns zero rows.

let me give you csv as well that was avalaible to download to confirm but i think you are correct.

4:35 PM
Ran 2 commands

That confirms it. The shapefile has 12 polygons: Plant #2 service area, Plant #3 service area, shared plant border, and several effluent disposal sites. Those four tracts with 32 rows are sitting across the boundary between Plant #2 and Plant #3 (or overlapping disposal site polygons).

Your fix is correct. After merging the fragments, those four tracts will each have one row with the summed overlap fraction. The effluent disposal sites might also be creating extra intersections for tracts that overlap them. You may want to filter the shapefile to only the actual service area polygons before the intersection:

r
sewer_boundary <- st_read("path/to/Sewer_Service_Area/") %>%
  filter(grepl("SEWER SERVICE AREA|SHARED PLANT BORDER", SSA_NAME))

That would keep only OBJECTIDs 1, 2, 11, and 12 (the actual service boundaries) and drop the effluent disposal sites, which are treatment facility parcels, not areas where households pay sewer bills. Then dissolve into a single polygon:

r
sewer_boundary <- sewer_boundary %>%
  st_union() %>%
  st_sf()

That way the intersection produces at most one fragment per tract and you avoid the duplication entirely. Re-run with that and the merge step shouldn't even be necessary.

4:39 PM

Good thinking on both points.

On the service area breakdown: keeping the undissolved version is smart. If Plant 2 is the one being replaced ($500-600M) and Plant 3 is being expanded, you could show whether the affordability burden falls differently across service areas. Some tracts might be served by Plant 2 only, others by Plant 3 only, and the shared border area by both. If lower-income tracts are concentrated in one plant's service area, that's a relevant finding.

On the Prop 218 question: you're touching on something important. Prop 218 requires that fees charged to property owners cannot exceed the proportional cost of the service provided to their property. The key legal principle is that fees must bear a reasonable relationship to the cost of service attributable to the parcel being charged.

Whether the city can charge all ratepayers for Plant 2 replacement depends on how the system operates. If Plants 2 and 3 function as an interconnected system (shared trunk lines, shared capacity, flows can be directed between plants), the city can treat it as one system and spread costs across all ratepayers. If they operate independently with separate collection systems serving distinct areas, then charging Plant 3 customers for Plant 2 replacement could be challenged under Prop 218.

I don't know enough about Bakersfield's sewer system configuration to answer that definitively. Do you know if the collection systems are interconnected, or if flows are routed to specific plants based on geography?

For now, I'd suggest: run the analysis both ways (dissolved for the overall story, undissolved to show per-plant-area results), and note the Prop 218 cost allocation question in the fact sheet as something the city should address transparently. That's a natural extension of the "guardrails" argument: if residents are being asked to pay for infrastructure that primarily serves other areas, that's another dimension of fairness beyond income.

Want to keep going with the analysis runs, or dig into the Prop 218 angle first?

This plant is located near Highway 58 and Mount Vernon (9:07). * Treatment Plant 3 (West): Services the area highlighted in green on the map (9:01). This plant is located near Ash and McCutchen, west of the 99 (13:47).

4:46 PM

This is really helpful. So the two plants serve distinct geographic areas split roughly by the 99 freeway, with Plant 2 covering east/southeast Bakersfield (purple) and Plant 3 covering west/southwest (green). The red box looks like the Plant 2 facility itself on Planz Road.

Looking at that map and knowing Bakersfield's income geography: east Bakersfield and the areas near Oildale, Oil City, and Edison (Plant 2's service area in purple) tend to have lower household incomes than the west side and southwest (Plant 3's service area in green, which includes Rosedale and the newer southwest developments).

So the $500-600M Plant 2 replacement primarily serves the lower-income side of town, while Plant 3 expansion serves the higher-income growth areas. That raises an interesting equity dimension: are lower-income households in the Plant 2 area subsidizing infrastructure expansion in the Plant 3 area, or vice versa? Under a uniform rate, everyone pays the same $875 regardless of which plant serves them.

For the analysis, keep the undissolved shapefile and run the affordability calculation separately for Plant 2 service area tracts vs. Plant 3 service area tracts. The code change in Script 02 would be to add a plant assignment column after the intersection in Script 01 (using SSA_NAME from the shapefile), then group the summary table by plant as well as scenario.

But I'd suggest keeping the Prop 218 cost allocation question as a discussion point in the fact sheet rather than making it a central argument. Your core message is clean and strong: the city needs to pair rate increases with affordability protections. The plant-level breakdown adds depth, but the cost allocation question gets legally complicated fast and could distract from the main ask. What do you think?

These numbers look solid. Let me summarize the final results:

140,089 households across 133 tracts. No tracts exceed 100%, the distributions are sensible, and the MHI vs. bracket comparison tells exactly the story you'd expect.

The headline numbers for the fact sheet:

ScenarioUnaffordable HH% of Total
Current ($247)6,2184.4%
FY 26/27 ($475)16,43411.7%
FY 30/31 ($875)33,73224.1%

The MHI comparison is striking: at FY 30/31, the MHI method flags only 13 tracts (8.8% of households) while the bracket method finds 24.1%. The bracket method catches nearly three times more affected households.

The burden by bracket confirms the pattern: households earning under $35,000 spend more than 2.5% on sewer at the $875 rate, and there are about 33,500 households in those bottom six brackets.

The fact sheet currently says ~29,100 households and 23.3%. The updated numbers with the actual service area boundary are 33,732 households and 24.1%. can you also explain me these two figures. fig 1 and 2

4:57 PM

Figure 1 answers the question: at each proposed rate step, how many households in the Bakersfield sewer service area would spend more than 2.5% of their income on sewer?

Each bar represents one year of the proposed rate schedule. At the current rate of $247/year, about 6,218 households (4.4%) already exceed the affordability threshold. These are the very lowest income households, those earning under roughly $10,000/year. As the rate climbs to $875 by FY 30/31, the threshold income rises to $35,000, meaning any household earning less than $35,000 now falls into the unaffordable category. That captures about 33,732 households, or nearly one in four.

The takeaway for a general audience: even before the rate increase, some households struggle. The proposed schedule doesn't just make things a little worse; it roughly quintuples the number of affected households.

Figure 2 answers the question: why does the method matter?

The red bars are the same numbers from Figure 1, using the income bracket method that counts actual households below the threshold in each tract. The blue bars use the traditional approach, which only looks at the tract's single median income number and asks whether the rate exceeds 2.5% of that median.

At FY 30/31, the blue bar shows 8.8% while the red bar shows 24.1%. The traditional method misses about two-thirds of the affected households. This happens because a tract with a median income of $50,000 looks "affordable" by the traditional method ($875 is 1.75% of $50,000), but that same tract still has households earning $15,000, $20,000, $30,000 who are paying well above the threshold. The bracket method counts those households individually instead of hiding them behind the median.

At the current rate, the blue bar is essentially zero because no tract in Bakersfield has a median income below $9,885. The traditional method says zero tracts have a problem. The bracket method finds 6,218 households already struggling.

this is based on EPA method? Fig1

4:58 PM

Yes. The 2.5% threshold comes from EPA guidance. Specifically, the EPA established 2.5% of median household income as the upper bound for drinking water affordability in the 1996 Safe Drinking Water Act framework. For wastewater, the EPA's 1997 Combined Sewer Overflows Financial Capability Assessment guidance uses 2.0% of MHI, and many studies combine the two for a 4.5% combined water and sewer threshold.

Maxcy-Brown et al. (2024) used the 2.5% threshold for wastewater in their Alabama study because most Alabama sewer utilities base wastewater bills on water consumption, making the 2.5% drinking water threshold more directly applicable. Your analysis follows the same choice, which is actually the more conservative (higher) threshold. If you used the 2.0% wastewater-specific threshold, even more households would be flagged as unaffordable.

One important clarification for the fact sheet: the EPA established these thresholds for evaluating a utility's financial capability for regulatory compliance, not specifically as a household-level affordability standard. Maxcy-Brown et al. adapted them to household-level analysis, which is the methodological contribution of their paper that your analysis builds on. The EPA itself has acknowledged that MHI-based thresholds don't capture household-level affordability well, which is why they released updated Financial Capability Assessment guidance in 2023.

So Figure 1 uses an EPA-derived threshold applied at the household level through the Maxcy-Brown method, rather than at the utility level as the EPA originally intended. That distinction is worth stating in the methods section, which the fact sheet already does. The approach is defensible and published in a peer-reviewed ASCE journal.

OKay so it is nto epa it is alabama method let's say one is MHI one is bracket method red is bracket method and blue is MHI based as whole. I want another plot that just shows blue bars

5:01 PM

Good call. You want a standalone figure showing just the MHI-based results so readers can see what the traditional approach finds on its own, before you show them what the bracket method reveals.

Add this to Script 03 after the Figure 2 section:

r
## --- Figure 2b: MHI-based only -----------------------------------------------

fig2b_data <- summary_table %>%
  mutate(
    scenario = factor(scenario, levels = c("Current", "FY 26/27", "FY 27/28",
                                            "FY 28/29", "FY 29/30", "FY 30/31")),
    mhi_based_pct = hh_in_mhi_unaffordable_tracts / total_hh * 100,
    label = paste0(round(mhi_based_pct, 1), "%\n(",
                   tracts_mhi_unaffordable, " tracts)")
  )

fig2b <- ggplot(fig2b_data, aes(x = scenario, y = mhi_based_pct)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#4393c3", alpha = 0.85, width = 0.65) +
  geom_text(aes(label = label), vjust = -0.5, size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "grey30") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 30),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15)),
    labels = scales::percent_format(scale = 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "MHI-Based Affordability Assessment",
    subtitle = "Households in tracts where sewer rate >= 2.5% of tract median household income",
    x = "Proposed Rate Schedule",
    y = "Households with Unaffordable Sewer Access (%)",
    caption = "Data: ACS 2019-2023 5-Year Estimates (Table B19013)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 10),
    plot.caption = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

ggsave("output/fig2b_mhi_only.png", fig2b,
       width = 8, height = 5, dpi = 300, bg = "white")

I set the y-axis limit to 30% so it's on the same scale as Figure 1, making the visual comparison obvious when you put them side by side. The labels show both the percentage and the number of tracts flagged. At FY 30/31 it should show 8.8% with 13 tracts, compared to 24.1% in Figure 1.

5:03 PM

You're right. Keep the same format as Figure 1 so they're directly comparable side by side.

r
## --- Figure 2b: MHI-based only (same format as Fig 1) ------------------------

fig2b_data <- summary_table %>%
  mutate(
    scenario = factor(scenario, levels = c("Current", "FY 26/27", "FY 27/28",
                                            "FY 28/29", "FY 29/30", "FY 30/31")),
    mhi_based_pct = hh_in_mhi_unaffordable_tracts / total_hh * 100,
    mhi_based_hh  = hh_in_mhi_unaffordable_tracts,
    label = paste0(round(mhi_based_pct, 1), "%\n(",
                   scales::comma(round(mhi_based_hh)), " HH)")
  )

fig2b <- ggplot(fig2b_data, aes(x = scenario, y = mhi_based_pct)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#4393c3", alpha = 0.85, width = 0.65) +
  geom_text(aes(label = label), vjust = -0.5, size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "grey30") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 30),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15)),
    labels = scales::percent_format(scale = 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Estimated Households with Unaffordable Sewer Access",
    subtitle = "City of Bakersfield | MHI-based method (traditional)",
    x = "Proposed Rate Schedule",
    y = "Households Exceeding Affordability Threshold (%)",
    caption = paste0(
      "Data: ACS 2019-2023 5-Year Estimates (Table B19013) | ",
      "Threshold: rate >= 2.5% of tract MHI\n",
      "Rate schedule from City of Bakersfield Prop 218 notice (Feb 2026)"
    )
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 10),
    plot.caption = element_text(color = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

ggsave("output/fig2b_mhi_only.png", fig2b,
       width = 8, height = 5, dpi = 300, bg = "white")

Same title, same y-axis label, same bar label format (% and HH count), same y-axis scale (0-30%) so when placed next to Figure 1 the difference jumps out immediately. Only the subtitle, color, and caption change to indicate the method.

Ran 3 commands, created a file

The issue is paragraph borders using left where the schema expects a different order. The figPlaceholder function uses paragraph-level left/right borders which docx-js outputs in wrong order. Let me simplify the placeholder to avoid paragraph borders.

Ran 2 commands, edited a file, read a file

Updated fact sheet with all corrected numbers and both methods explained. Here's what changed from the previous version:

The new section "Two Ways to Measure Who Is Affected" walks through both methods in plain language. It presents the MHI results first (8.8% at FY 30/31, 13 tracts), then the bracket results (24.1%, ~33,700 households), then explains why they differ with the $50,000-median tract example. Each method gets its own results table and figure placeholder.

Figure placeholders are marked with dashed-border boxes labeled with which figure goes where. You have six spots to fill: Figure 6b (income-normalized comparison), Figure 2b (MHI-based blue bars), Figure 1 (bracket-based red bars), Figure 2 (side-by-side comparison), Figure 7 (sensitivity lines), plus the maps if you want them.

The MHI results table uses approximate numbers from the summary output (I back-calculated from the percentages and total HH). Double-check those against your actual hh_in_mhi_unaffordable_tracts column.

i like this Bakersfield's Sewer System Needs Upgrades. So Do Its Protections for Vulnerable Communities. but this part seemed weird So Do Its Protections

5:17 PM

How about:

"Bakersfield's Sewer System Needs Upgrades. So Do Protections for Its Most Vulnerable Communities."

Dropping "Its" from before "Protections" and moving it to "Its Most Vulnerable Communities" reads more naturally and puts the emphasis on the people rather than the policy mechanism.

so Bakersfield's Sewer System this seems okay but there who are we askign about So Do Protections for

5:18 PM

Good point. "So Do" implies someone already has protections that need upgrading, but there are no protections in place at all. The city hasn't done any affordability analysis or adopted any assistance programs.

How about:

"Bakersfield's Sewer System Needs Upgrades, and Its Most Vulnerable Communities Need Protections."

That makes a clean parallel: the system needs something, the communities need something. No ambiguity about who is being asked or what exists already.

i also found this form youtube According to the presentation, state law requires that all rate payers pay for the services they receive, meaning the city cannot charge different rates to different individuals based on income (42:05-42:28). However, the City Council could determine to create a program using other general tax resources to assist those struggling to pay their utility bills (42:31-42:40).Additionally, the city is looking into moderate rate increases that start lower and increase gradually over time to make the payments more manageable (42:58-43:11).

5:23 PM

That's an important detail. The city is aware of the affordability concern and has already acknowledged two things: they can't do income-based sewer rates under Prop 218, but they can create assistance programs funded from general tax revenue. They're also considering more gradual phase-ins.

This actually strengthens your fact sheet because:

  1. The city itself has acknowledged the need for protections, so you're not asking for something radical. You're providing the data that shows how many households need it and pushing them to follow through.
  2. The "we can't charge different rates" framing is technically correct under Prop 218 for the sewer fee itself, but the city has other tools. The general fund assistance program they mentioned is exactly what the Water Environment Federation recommends. Other cities in California do this already.
  3. The gradual phase-in they mentioned is what ended up in the current proposal ($475 stepping up to $875) after they scrapped the original 300% jump to $950. Your sensitivity analysis shows that even with the gradual approach, the endpoint still hits 24% of households.

Do you want me to update the "What Needs to Happen" section of the fact sheet to reference this? It would be stronger to acknowledge that the city has already recognized the need and discussed solutions, and frame your analysis as providing the data to guide those decisions rather than introducing a new idea. Something like: the city has indicated it could create an assistance program using general tax resources, and this analysis provides the household-level data needed to design and target such a program effectively.

yeah i dont want to be one sayign they are bad i just want them to prepare they dont have number onhow tbifg this problem will be and they should know that.

5:24 PM

That's the perfect tone. You're not adversarial, you're providing the analysis they don't have so they can make informed decisions. Let me update the title and the "What Needs to Happen" section.

Edited 3 files, ran 2 commands, read a file

The tone is now collaborative throughout. The key changes:

The "What Needs to Happen" section now leads by acknowledging what the city has already done right (recognizing the concern, considering gradual phase-in, discussing general fund assistance). It frames your analysis as filling a gap: the city knows there's a concern but doesn't have the numbers. You're providing those numbers so they can plan effectively.

The closing line ties it together: investing in infrastructure and investing in community protections aren't competing priorities.

The section header changed from "Customer Assistance Programs" to "Options the City Can Consider," which reads less prescriptive.

Bakersfield sewer affordability factsheet

i want to tone down on this Proposed Rate Schedule Fiscal Year Annual Rate Monthly Equiv. Income Below Which Sewer Is Unaffordable Current (FY 25/26) $247 $21/mo $9,885 FY 26/27 $475 $40/mo $19,000 FY 27/28 $575 $48/mo $23,000 FY 28/29 $675 $56/mo $27,000 FY 29/30 $775 $65/mo $31,000 FY 30/31 $875 $73/mo $35,000 The last column shows the household income below which the sewer charge exceeds 2.5% of annual income. This threshold comes from U.S. Environmental Protection Agency guidance used to evaluate whether wastewater costs place an unreasonable burden on households. The Problem with Comparing Dollar Amounts On its website, the city presents a chart comparing Bakersfield’s sewer rate to other California cities. At $247 per year, Bakersfield’s current rate is the lowest on the chart. The message is that even after increases, rates remain below those of cities like Sacramento, Los Angeles, and San Francisco. This comparison is misleading because it ignores a basic fact: Bakersfield residents earn less than residents of most of those cities. A $900 sewer bill means something very different to a household earning $136,000 in San Francisco than to a household earning $50,000 in east Bakersfield. The table below uses the same cities from the city’s comparison chart but adds median household income and calculates the sewer charge as a percentage of that income. Rate Comparison Adjusted for Income City Annual Rate Median HH Income Sewer as % of MHI Bakersfield (current) $247 $82,093 0.30% Bakersfield (FY 30/31) $875 $82,093 1.07% Fresno $309 $66,804 0.46% City of Shafter $413 $67,989 0.61% City of Arvin $727 $49,984 1.45% Sacramento $832 $76,825 1.08% Los Angeles $910 $75,424 1.21% San Francisco $2,000 $136,397 1.47% Sources: Sewer rates from City of Bakersfield website (FY 2025-26). Median household income from U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey 2019-2023 5-Year Estimates. When income is considered, Bakersfield at the proposed $875 rate carries a burden (1.07% of MHI) similar to Sacramento (1.08%) and higher than Fresno (0.46%). The comparison to San Francisco, which the city highlights, obscures that San Francisco residents earn roughly 66% more than Bakersfield residents. You're right. Let me soften this section. Instead of "misleading" and "obscures," frame it as: the comparison is useful but incomplete, and adding income context gives a fuller picture. We're not saying they're wrong, we're saying there's more to the story.

The data still speaks for itself, but the framing is constructive rather than confrontational.
